assumptions: Cold air mass persists for the full 10-day window without premature moderation
Production does not accelerate meaningfully to offset the demand increase
Storage inventory remains below the 5-year average heading into the event
counterarguments: Forecasts at 6-10 days carry substantial uncertainty; the cold snap may be shorter or less intense
If managed-money positioning is already long, price may not react as strongly to the fundamentals
LNG export demand could soften if European buyers reduce purchases in response to a price spike
data_needed_to_validate: NWS 6-10 day temperature anomaly map — update Thursday morning
EIA weekly storage report Thursday 10:30 ET
Daily dry gas production estimate from Platts or equivalent
Henry Hub basis vs. Transco Zone 6 — watches for Northeast demand pull
result.narrative: The scenario suggests a moderately bullish directional pressure on Henry Hub over a 1-to-3-week timeframe, with the primary driver being elevated residential and commercial heating demand in the Northeast and a secondary contribution from Midwest heating load. The expected percentage range of 4–11% reflects uncertainty about the intensity and duration of the cold event; forecast models at the 6-10 day horizon are less reliable than at shorter ranges. Contradicting this view is the possibility that managed-money positioning is already net long at elevated levels, which historically limits additional upside momentum. Validation requires confirmation from updated NWS anomaly maps and the next EIA storage report.

name: LNG Export Disruption — Gulf Coast 14 Days

assumptions: The outage lasts the full 14 days without early restoration
Domestic demand is average for the season — no compensating cold weather demand
The gas that would have been exported remains in domestic circulation
counterarguments: Partial restoration within the first week would shrink the bearish impact considerably
If the outage is already widely known, price may have already adjusted
Any concurrent cold snap would offset or reverse the directional pressure
data_needed_to_validate: Operator communications or FERC filings confirming outage duration
LNG cargo tracking data showing reduced feedgas nominations
EIA storage report for evidence of reduced export pulls on inventory
result.narrative: A 2.1 Bcfd reduction in LNG feedgas demand over 14 days reads as moderately bearish for front-month Henry Hub prices, as the displaced gas adds incremental supply pressure to the domestic market. The directional pressure is contingent on weather remaining near seasonal norms; any concurrent cold event could neutralize or reverse the impact. The expected range of -2% to -8% is wide, reflecting uncertainty about how quickly the market prices in the restoration timeline. Managed-money positioning and storage levels at the time of the disruption are the key moderating variables.

assumptions: Wellhead freeze-offs occur at -18°F surface temperatures in Appalachian basin wells
Pipeline constraints prevent rapid rerouting of Midcontinent supply to the Northeast
The freeze-off lasts the full 7 days without interim thaw
counterarguments: Modern wellhead heating equipment reduces freeze-off risk relative to historical events
Higher current storage levels (relative to this scenario's baseline) dampen the price response
The 7-day duration is short enough that power generators may switch fuels before a significant storage draw
data_needed_to_validate: Real-time dry gas production estimates from Platts or BloombergNEF
Transcontinental Gas Pipe Line (Transco) Zone 5 and 6 daily nominations
NWS point forecasts for key Appalachian producing counties (Susquehanna, Tioga, Potter, PA)
result.narrative: This scenario combines a severe cold snap with physical production impairment, resulting in high-confidence bullish directional pressure over a 1-to-2-week window. The simultaneous heating demand surge and supply reduction create a self-reinforcing tightness that is more compelling than either factor alone. The expected range of 7–22% reflects the historical volatility of similar events; the upper bound assumes storage inventory is already below average and managed-money positioning adds momentum to the fundamental move. The primary counterargument is that modern wellhead infrastructure has reduced freeze-off risk since the most severe historical precedents.

assumptions: The hurricane makes direct landfall in key Gulf Coast production areas
Offshore platforms require 5 days for evacuation and production shut-in
Post-storm production restoration follows a typical historical ramp profile
counterarguments: Hurricane impacts during summer typically coincide with lower-demand shoulder season, limiting the net effect
Storage is typically near seasonal highs in August–September, providing a buffer
Production restores faster than assumed if storm damage is limited to shutting in rather than physical destruction
data_needed_to_validate: National Hurricane Center track and intensity forecast
BSEE shut-in reports from Gulf of Mexico operators
Post-storm pipeline and LNG terminal damage assessment reports
result.narrative: Hurricane scenarios produce wide outcome ranges because the market's response depends critically on storm track, intensity, and the coincidence with demand conditions. This scenario suggests a modestly bullish directional pressure with low confidence, as the summer seasonal context — higher storage and lower heating demand — partially offsets the supply disruption. The -2% lower bound reflects the possibility that the market had already partially priced in weather risk before the storm made landfall. The 14% upper bound assumes direct infrastructure damage beyond a simple production shut-in.

name: Geopolitical Shock — European LNG Demand Spike

assumptions: US LNG export infrastructure can absorb the incremental feedgas demand without operational constraints
The geopolitical disruption persists long enough for multiple LNG cargoes to reload before resolution
Domestic demand remains near seasonal norms — no concurrent cold weather event
counterarguments: US LNG export capacity is near physical limits; incremental feedgas demand may not translate to incremental throughput
If European buyers have contracted storage, spot demand may be less than the headline disruption implies
Resolution of the geopolitical event within the first week would limit the duration of demand pressure
data_needed_to_validate: Daily LNG cargo loading data from European AIS tracking services
Henry Hub basis vs. Title Transfer Facility (TTF) spread to verify arbitrage remains open
Weekly EIA storage report showing larger-than-normal draw attributable to export feedgas
result.narrative: A sustained increase in European LNG feedgas demand reads as moderately bullish for Henry Hub over a 3-to-6-week window, with the key transmission mechanism being an incremental draw on domestic storage inventory. The expected range of 6–18% is consistent with historical responses to similar demand-pull events. The primary risk to this view is US LNG physical capacity: if the terminals are already operating near nameplate capacity, the incremental demand translates to higher prices without additional cargo throughput. The TTF-Henry Hub spread is the most reliable real-time indicator of whether the arbitrage is open and driving actual export decisions.
